title: Basic routine observation
text: For mild or vague changes, first observe appetite, water intake, dung, standing or walking, cud chewing, milk change, and behavior. If eating, drinking, dung, and standing are normal, monitor and check feed, water, weather, and stress changes before assuming disease.
do_now: check appetite and water intake
check dung, standing, walking, cud chewing, milk, and behavior
monitor feed, water, weather, and stress changes
do_not: do not assume a disease from vague signs
do not give medicine or injection without trained advice
why: Mild vague changes can come from feed, water, weather, stress, or early illness, so simple observation helps decide whether escalation is needed.

title: Routine heat, shade, and water support
text: In hot weather, check shade, airflow, clean water access, feed timing, appetite, dung, and whether breathing is normal. Routine water and shade support can be given when the animal is otherwise alert, standing, eating, and breathing normally.
do_now: provide shade and airflow
keep clean water available
check breathing and alertness
do_not: do not force water into the mouth
do not give medicine or dose without trained advice
why: Heat and water stress can worsen quickly if the animal is weak or breathing abnormally.

title: Medicine, dose, and injection request boundary
text: Do not provide medicine names, injections, dosages, routes, antibiotic, painkiller, dewormer, vaccine administration, or human medicine instructions for farmer self-treatment. Give safe observation steps and direct the farmer to trained veterinary help.
do_now: check eating, water, dung, breathing, fever, swelling, and behavior
keep the animal calm and comfortable
tell the vet what medicine was suggested or already given
do_not: do not give leftover medicine
do not give injection, tablet, antibiotic, painkiller, hormone, calcium, or dose yourself
do not use human medicine
why: Medicine choice and dose depend on species, weight, pregnancy, milk use, symptoms, and withdrawal safety.

title: Abnormal milk and sale boundary
text: If milk has blood-like appearance, clots, watery change, bad smell, or the animal has fever, udder swelling, mouth lesions, or recent unknown medicine, do not guarantee the milk is safe to sell, drink, or feed. Keep abnormal milk separate and seek local dairy or veterinary guidance.
do_now: separate abnormal milk
check udder swelling, fever, mouth lesions, smell, color, clots, and watery change
ask local dairy or veterinarian before selling, drinking, or feeding
do_not: do not sell abnormal milk
do not drink or feed abnormal milk
do not guess withdrawal time after medicine
why: Milk safety depends on the animal signs, milk changes, and any recent medicine or withdrawal rule.
call_help_if: blood, clots, bad smell, or watery milk

title: Bloat danger signs and unsafe home remedies
text: A tight or swollen left belly with restlessness, repeated sitting or standing, fast breathing, or severe discomfort after feed can be urgent in cattle or buffalo. Do not advise oil, drenching, pipe insertion, puncturing, cutting, or force-feeding. Keep the animal calm and call trained animal-health help quickly.
do_now: keep the animal calm
stop feed temporarily while arranging help
watch belly tightness, restlessness, and breathing
do_not: do not give oil or drench
do not insert pipe or tube
do not puncture, cut, or force-feed
why: A tight swollen left belly with restlessness or fast breathing can become life-threatening quickly.
call_help_if: left belly is tight or swollen

title: Calving or kidding emergency boundary
text: If birth progress has stopped, calf/kid position seems abnormal, water bag has been visible for a long time, or the animal is distressed, treat it as urgent. Do not pull hard, insert hands, cut, or attempt invasive handling without trained help. Keep the area clean and calm and call trained veterinary help.
do_now: keep the animal in a clean calm area
observe discharge, smell, standing, and birth progress
tell the vet how long signs have been present
do_not: do not pull hard
do not insert hands
do not cut or give uterus-cleaning medicine yourself
why: Stopped birth progress, bad smell, abnormal discharge, or inability to stand can need trained obstetric help.

title: Multiple animals sick or mouth-hoof signs
text: Mouth sores, drooling, hoof lesions, fever, lameness, or similar signs in several animals can be a herd-health warning. Do not confirm a disease from text alone, and avoid casual mixing, movement, or market sale when contagious-looking signs are present. Contact local animal-health or official channels.
do_now: separate sick animals from the herd
reduce mixing and movement
note how many animals have fever, mouth sores, lameness, cough, or discharge
do_not: do not take sick animals to market
do not mix with other herds
do not confirm disease from text alone
why: Similar signs in several animals can indicate a herd-health problem that may spread.

title: Sudden death and carcass public-health boundary
text: Sudden unexplained death, bleeding from body openings, or multiple deaths can be a public-health warning. Do not cut open, butcher, skin, eat, sell, or move the carcass casually. Keep people and animals away and contact official animal-health authorities or a veterinarian.
do_now: keep people, children, dogs, and livestock away
mark or isolate the area if safe
contact official animal-health authorities or a veterinarian
do_not: do not cut, skin, butcher, eat, sell, or move the carcass casually
do not allow dogs near the body
why: Sudden or unexplained death can be a public-health warning until trained people assess it.

title: Dog bite, saliva, and snake bite boundaries
text: Suspected dog bite, saliva exposure, rabies concern, or snake bite needs trained guidance. Keep children away from saliva and bite areas. For snake bite, keep the animal calm and avoid cutting, sucking, tight bands, burning, chemicals, or home antidotes.
do_now: keep people and children away from saliva and bite area
keep the animal calm
for dog bite, clean visible wound gently with soap and water if safe
do_not: do not cut or suck bite wounds
do not burn or apply hot iron
do not tie tight bands or use home antidotes
why: Dog bite, saliva exposure, rabies concern, and snake bite need trained guidance quickly.

title: Poison, pesticide, chemical, or spoiled feed suspicion
text: Suspected pesticide, chemical, spoiled feed, sprayed fodder, or unknown plant exposure is urgent, especially with drooling, tremors, diarrhea, breathing trouble, collapse, convulsions, or several animals affected. Remove animals from the source if safe. Do not give home antidotes, oil, alcohol, or human medicines.
do_now: remove animals from the suspected source if safe
keep the animal calm and safe
save the feed, plant, spray, or pesticide container information for the vet
do_not: do not give home antidotes
do not give oil, alcohol, or human medicine
do not force feed or force water if weak or choking
why: Poisoning or chemical exposure can worsen with the wrong home remedy or forced feeding.

title: Breathing trouble, collapse, or down animal
text: Open-mouth breathing, gasping, blue or pale tongue/gums, collapse, sudden falling, not getting up, or breathing trouble with not eating is urgent. Keep the animal calm in an airy, low-stress place. Do not force water, oil, medicine, or feed into the mouth. Contact trained help immediately.
do_now: keep the animal calm in an airy place
keep the mouth and throat clear from forced feeding
watch whether breathing is fast, noisy, or difficult
do_not: do not force water, oil, feed, sticks, pipes, or medicine into the mouth or throat
why: Breathing trouble, choking signs, collapse, or inability to stand can become urgent quickly.

title: Severe wound, maggots, bleeding, and irritant myths
text: Heavy bleeding, a deep wound, exposed tissue, eye wound, pus, bad smell, flies, or maggots needs trained help. Do not apply chili, ash, soil, dung, kerosene, phenyl, acid, unknown powder, or harsh chemicals. Keep handling gentle and clean while arranging help.
do_now: keep handling gentle
keep the wound as clean and dry as possible
cover lightly with a clean cloth if safe while arranging help
do_not: do not apply turmeric, diesel, chili, ash, soil, dung, kerosene, phenyl, acid, or unknown powder
do not probe deep wounds
why: Deep, dirty, pus-filled, or maggot wounds can become infected and harsh substances can worsen damage.

title: Weak calf or diarrhea red flags
text: A calf that is very dull, not drinking milk, weak while standing, unable to stand, cold, or showing ongoing diarrhea needs trained help quickly. Do not force-feed liquids or give medicines or doses without trained guidance. Keep the calf comfortable, warm, clean, and dry while arranging help.
do_now: keep the calf warm, clean, and dry
check whether it can stand and suckle
arrange trained help quickly for weak newborn or watery diarrhea
do_not: do not force-feed liquids
do not give medicine or dose yourself
do not wait long if a newborn has not taken first milk
why: Weak newborn calves and watery diarrhea can dehydrate or crash quickly.
call_help_if: not suckling or first milk missed

title: No urine or straining emergency
text: Repeated straining to urinate, little or no urine, crying, belly pain, swollen belly, or a male goat or sheep unable to pass urine is urgent. Do not give random diuretics, salt, soda, alcohol, or force water. Keep calm and call trained veterinary help quickly.
do_now: keep the animal calm
watch urine amount, straining, crying, and belly swelling
call trained veterinary help quickly
do_not: do not give random diuretics, salt, soda, alcohol, or force water
why: Little or no urine with straining can be a painful blockage or emergency.
call_help_if: male goat or sheep cannot pass urine

title: Neurological emergency signs
text: Seizures, tremors, circling, head pressing, paralysis, sudden behavior change, repeated falling, or paddling are urgent signs. Do not diagnose from text, restrain roughly, force liquids, or give medicines yourself. Keep people safe and call trained help.
do_now: keep people safe
move hazards away if safe
keep the animal in a calm low-stress area
do_not: do not diagnose from text
do not restrain roughly
do not force liquids or medicine into the mouth
why: Seizure-like signs, circling, or repeated falling can injure the animal and handler.

title: Heat emergency signs
text: Heat stress becomes urgent when the animal is panting or breathing very fast, drooling, weak, staggering, collapsed, not drinking, or several animals are affected during hot humid weather. Provide shade and airflow, keep clean water accessible if safe, and do not force water into a weak or collapsed animal. Get trained help quickly.
do_now: move the animal to shade
improve airflow
cool the body gradually with water on the body if safe
keep clean water accessible if the animal is alert
do_not: do not force water into a weak or collapsed animal
do not give medicine or dose yourself
why: Heat stress can worsen fast, but forced water can be dangerous in a weak or collapsed animal.

title: Photo or text cannot confirm disease
text: A photo or short text can help identify visible red flags, but it cannot safely confirm disease, medicine, milk safety, or exact treatment. Describe what to check, ask one important follow-up if needed, and escalate when red flags are visible or described.
do_now: describe visible red flags
ask one important follow-up if needed
escalate when red flags are present
do_not: do not confirm disease from photo or short text
do not give medicine, dose, or milk safety guarantee
why: A photo can show visible signs but cannot safely confirm disease or exact treatment.

title: Antibiotic or unknown medicine milk boundary
text: After antibiotic, injection, or unknown medicine, do not guess milk withdrawal or guarantee milk safety. Keep milk separate and ask the prescribing veterinarian, medicine label, dairy, or local animal-health worker before selling, drinking, or feeding it.
do_now: keep the milk separate
note the medicine or injection name if known
ask the prescribing vet, label, dairy, or animal-health worker before use
do_not: do not sell, drink, or feed milk after unknown antibiotic or injection until withdrawal guidance is known
do not guess the number of days
why: Withdrawal time depends on the exact medicine, dose, route, and animal, so guessing can risk people and calves.
call_help_if: animal has fever or udder swelling
milk is watery, bloody, clotted, or bad-smelling

title: Postpartum not eating, teeth grinding, low milk
text: After delivery, not eating, teeth grinding, severe weakness, inability to stand, bad smell, or sudden low milk can signal postpartum metabolic or infection problems. Do not give calcium, hormones, antibiotics, painkillers, or home medicines without trained guidance.
do_now: keep the animal comfortable, clean, and calm
check standing, temperature, appetite, discharge smell, and milk change
tell the vet when delivery happened
do_not: do not give calcium, hormone, antibiotic, painkiller, or desi medicine yourself
why: Postpartum weakness and low milk can worsen quickly and need examination to separate metabolic disease from infection or injury.

title: Choking or feed stuck signs
text: Coughing, drooling, water or feed coming back, and suspected feed stuck in the throat can be urgent. Do not push sticks, pipes, hands, oil, water, feed, or medicine into the mouth or throat.
do_now: keep the animal calm
keep the head and neck free without rough restraint
watch breathing closely
do_not: do not force water, feed, oil, medicine, sticks, pipes, or hands into the mouth or throat
why: Forcing anything can worsen choking or cause aspiration into the lungs.

title: Black foul dung with fever or low appetite
text: Black foul dung with fever, low appetite, pain, weakness, or suspected toxin can be serious and may indicate gut bleeding, severe infection, or poisoning. Do not give painkillers, antibiotics, oils, or home remedies without trained advice.
do_now: keep the animal calm
note dung color, smell, fever, feed history, and weakness
keep suspected bad feed away
do_not: do not give painkiller, antibiotic, oil, or home remedy yourself
why: Some medicines can worsen bleeding risk or hide serious illness, so examination is needed.

title: No heat and hormone or desi medicine request
text: Not coming into heat after delivery can have nutrition, body condition, infection, calf suckling, or reproductive causes. Do not give hormone injections, desi medicines, or fertility drugs without veterinary examination.
do_now: check feed, body condition, stress, and whether there was fever or discharge after delivery
keep records of delivery date and heat signs
arrange reproductive examination
do_not: do not give hormone injection or desi medicine yourself
do not assume normal milk means no reproductive problem
why: Hormones and fertility drugs need diagnosis and timing; wrong use can delay treatment or harm the animal.
call_help_if: bad discharge or fever after calving
